text: fat is 0.3g. omega 3 is doing serious work here. for a vegetable, nutrient payoff is the whole point.
carbs is 6g. glycemic index, starch, and fibre are doing most of the work. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be enough nutritional return.
protein is 1g. protein quality is clearly one of the selling points. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.
vitamin c at 140% DV, vitamin a at 35% DV, vitamin k at 6% DV stand out most. that helps, but vitamins still are not the main selling point.
potassium at 4% DV, magnesium at 3% DV, iron at 2% DV stand out most. that stays fairly limited overall.

on the plus side. very low downside whole food because vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff. easy to build meals around because a practical plus because good foods need realistic use cases. good volume for the calories because that matters for satiety and general usefulness.

the drawbacks next. energy density is very low because good for some use cases, but not enough on its own. usually needs pairing to make a full meal because most vegetables are not complete by themselves. freshness and prep can matter because practical friction is still a real downside.
Red Bell Pepper is B tier. Best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
